results.caption:
They didn't just build ransomware. They built a brand.

DragonForce launched on BreachForums on December 6, 2023 — not as a tool, but as a cartel with a logo, a revenue model, and a mythology.

363 victims. 30+ countries. £300M+ in documented damage.

This is what happens when ransomware operators start thinking like franchise CEOs.

The dragon isn't just a mascot. It's a warning.

#DragonForce #RansomwareAsAService #ThreatIntelligence #CyberSecurity #RaaS
results.hook: A mythologized threat actor origin visual that transforms dry threat intel into a dragon-mythology brand identity piece — making cybersecurity professionals stop scrolling.

results.topic: The Binary Signature — Ransom Note Decoded
results.caption:
This is what your team sees at 3AM.

A DragonForce ransom note. ChaCha8 encryption. ESXi hosts locked. EDR killed via BYOVD — truesight.sys and rentdrv2.sys weaponized against your own defenses.

Marks & Spencer. Co-op. Harrods. £300M+ in damages across one summer.

The note isn't the end of the attack. It's the receipt.

Know the signature before it lands in your inbox.

#DragonForce #Ransomware #IncidentResponse #DFIR #CyberThreatIntelligence
results.hook: Placing a real-context ransomware note reconstruction inside a forensic lab scene makes abstract malware technical — visceral, urgent, and shareable to every SOC professional and CISO on LinkedIn.

results.caption:
Nobody knows who runs DragonForce.

Malaysian hacktivist roots? A rebrand? A front for something larger? The cartel absorbed RansomHub on April 8, 2025 — growing faster than most threat intel teams can track.

Scattered Spider collaboration. Vishing campaigns. 52% of victims in the US alone.

When attribution fails, defenders lose their most important tool: context.

The dragon has no confirmed face. That's the point.

#ThreatIntelligence #DragonForce #AttributionGap #CyberEspionage #RedTeam
results.hook: The unresolved attribution mystery of DragonForce — shown as competing intelligence threads converging on a redacted identity — triggers the professional paranoia that makes cybersecurity content go viral.
